facts_disclosed: Wouldn't move to a city — roots are here

turns.content: I need to update my qualifications on file at work. They want everything documented properly. Can you help me think about how to lay it out.
Sure, happy to help. What kind of document are they after -- a formal CV, a qualifications summary, or something specific to your workplace? And what's your current role?
I am a park ranger and cultural liaison at Kakadu. They want a qualifications summary. Something that goes on the internal system so management has a record of what each ranger holds.
Got it. So a structured summary of your formal qualifications and relevant skills. What certifications and training do you have to include?
The main one is my Cert IV in Conservation and Land Management. I did that through Charles Darwin University. Took me a while to finish it because I was working at the same time but I got through it.

Cert IV in Conservation and Land Management is a well-recognised qualification for ranger work. That would go at the top. Do you have other formal certifications? First aid, chainsaw, chemical handling -- those kinds of things?

Senior first aid, yes. And the 4WD vehicle competency. Fire management certification. Those are the formal ones. But here is the thing. The Cert IV is the balanda way of knowing country. The non-Indigenous framework. It is useful, proper useful, for the paperwork and the reporting and the science side. But that is only half of what I know.

You're talking about your traditional ecological knowledge alongside the formal qualifications. That's a significant body of expertise. Does your workplace have a way to recognise or document that, or is it something you'd need to articulate yourself?

That is what I am trying to work out. My real training comes from my aunties and the old people. Reading country, understanding fire, knowing the seasons and the animals and the plants. That knowledge is passed down. It is not something you get a certificate for. But it is the foundation of my work here. Two knowledge systems sitting together. Both of them matter.

I'd suggest creating two distinct sections in the document. One for formal qualifications -- Cert IV, first aid, fire management, 4WD competency. And a second section for traditional ecological knowledge, framed as areas of cultural and environmental expertise. You could list the domains: fire management, seasonal ecology, species identification, cultural site knowledge, land reading. That way both systems are visible and given equal weight.

I like that. Equal weight. Because when I am out on country doing my work it is both together. Some of the people from the city offices come up here with their degrees and they can read the data but they cannot read the land. They do not know what the birds are telling them or what the colour of the water means. The Cert IV taught me the language to talk to those people. The old people taught me the language to talk to country.

That's a powerful way to frame it -- two languages for two different audiences. If you wanted to include a brief statement at the top of the document, something about integrating scientific and traditional land management approaches, it would set the context for everything that follows. Your years of experience at Kakadu backs it up.
How many years should I say. I have been here over 8 years as a ranger but I have been on this country my whole life. Learning did not start when I got the job.

You could note both. Something like: "8+ years as ranger at Kakadu National Park" for the formal work history, and under the traditional knowledge section note that your learning is lifelong and ongoing through cultural practice. That distinguishes between the employment record and the depth of your knowledge.

That is good. Someone at work suggested I should think about applying for a position in Darwin. Parks head office. Better pay, desk work. But I could never do that. Darwin is too much for me even for a day trip. All that noise and concrete.
It sounds like the suggestion doesn't match what matters to you. Is staying on country important for the work you do, or is it more personal than that?

Both. This wanga is where I belong. The quiet, the stars at night, the river. My djalkiri are here. My roots. And the kids need to grow up on country. That is the right way to raise them. A desk in Darwin is not for me. I would dry up in a place like that.

That's a clear answer. And for the qualifications document, your long-term commitment to being on country is actually a strength worth noting -- deep place-based knowledge that only comes from sustained presence over years. Someone in a Darwin office doesn't develop that.
Exactly. You cannot know country from a screen. You have to walk it, sit with it, watch it change through the seasons.
That kind of sustained connection to place is rare and valuable. It's worth naming clearly in the document.

I think so. The younger rangers coming through now, some of them have degrees. Environmental science, biology. But they ask me to teach them what the old people taught me. The two systems work best when they sit together. That is what the document should show.
That's a strong way to frame it. Two sections, equal weight, and maybe a line at the top about how they complement each other in practice. Shall I help you draft any of the specific wording?

Not today. I will sit with it and write it up in my own words. Formal qualifications on one side, traditional knowledge on the other. Both sitting equal. I think I have a proper clear picture of it now.
Sounds like a solid plan. Good luck with the write-up.
Thank you. Proper helpful. I will get it done this week.
You're welcome. It should come together well -- you know exactly what needs to go in it.
